questions.description: "The different circumstances in which the Courts have disregarded the separate legal personality of companies are many…
questions.text: "The different circumstances in which the Courts have disregarded the separate legal personality of companies are many and varied." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case law in your answer.

questions.description: Emma and Sarah are the directors and equal shareholders of The Shoe Shop Limited (the "Company").
questions.text: Emma and Sarah are the directors and equal shareholders of The Shoe Shop Limited (the "Company"). The Company operated a small chain of four shoe shops which has been in business selling ladies' fashion shoes for upwards of eight years. They approach you for advice in relation to the following matters: (a) The Company's objects clause is rather narrow and provides that the main object is "for the retail sale of ladies' fashion shoes". While there are many other powers specified in the objects clause, there is no "independent objects clause". Emma and Sarah have been considering diversifying and they eventually decided to sell fashion clothing for ladies as well as shoes. They ask whether the sale of such clothing is intra vires the Company. (b) The Company's objects clause provides that the Company has the capacity "to make gratuitous payments of up to €20,000 to any shareholder of the Company". Emma and Sarah want to know whether this can be relied upon to pay both of them €10,000 each from the company's capital. The Company has no distributable profits. You are required to answer both (a) [and]{.underline} (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air… Fiduciary, Company.
questions.text: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the "Company") which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The Company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from a company owned 50:50 by David and his brother Bart, called DB Limited, which has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise Joan and David on the proposed sale (or lease) of the mountain to the Company from DB Limited, having regard to [each]{.underline} of the following: (a) Section 194 of the Companies Act 1963, (b) Sections 29 and 31 of the Companies Act 1990, and (c) Their common law fiduciary duties as directors of the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Retail Motors Limited (the "Company") is in dire financial straits. Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Retail Motors Limited (the "Company") is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors, John and Timothy, have realised that the Company's used-car business is hopelessly insolvent.The Company owes €750,000 to the Revenue Commissioners in VAT, PAYE and PRSI; it owes €200,000 on its bank overdraft and also owes in the aggregate €500,000 to other creditors, compromised of suppliers and customers. The Company's assets are €275,000 comprised of cars worth approximately €250,000 and about €25,000 in cash. John and Timothy have been dodging their creditors all winter, but two of them have been particularly tenacious, one a customer and the other a trade creditor. The customer (Fred) is owed €15,000 for a car which he sold to the Company. Fred, who is an old school friend of Timothy, had been telephoning him and calling around to his house on a nightly basis seeking payment. Eventually Fred caught up with Timothy and after a heated conversation, when Fred actually pushed Timothy against the wall and warned him of the consequences of not paying him his money, Timothy capitulated and paid Fred the €15,000 owed to him. On being told by Timothy of what had happened, John decided that they must put the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation, and they initiated this the following day when a resolution was passed to place the company into liquidation. The trade creditor was a firm that had previously carried out repair work to used cars for the Company. This firm was owed €40,000 and its Managing Director (Mike) had become increasingly abusive to John and Timothy and most recently to Timothy's wife June whom he badly frightened after he made a telephone call to their home. In consequence of this, the day after the resolution was passed to put the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation, Timothy gave a cheque to Mike, drawn on the company's already overdrawn bank account. You are required to advise the Company's liquidator on the validity of the two payments made by the Company: (a) The cash payment of €15,000 to Fred, and (b) The cheque for €40,000 to Mike's firm. You are required to answer both (a) [and]{.underline} (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Unless it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court that the directors of an insolvent company have acted both…
questions.text: "Unless it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court that the directors of an insolvent company have acted both honestly and responsibly in relation to the affairs of that company, they will be restricted pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990." You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: In what circumstances will a floating charge crystallise?
questions.text: In what circumstances will a floating charge crystallise? In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing, and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise John as to the following matters: (a) Whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; and (b) Whether your answer would be different if the articles of association [or]{.underline} a shareholders' agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. You are required to answer both (a) [and]{.underline} (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to…
questions.text: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to transfer its shares, which restriction will usually take the form of either a right of pre-emption on transfer or a discretion vested in the directors to refuse the registration of share transfers". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case law in your answer. March 2013

questions.description: "Section 8 of the Companies Act 1963 and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 provide only limited protection to persons…
questions.text: "Section 8 of the Companies Act 1963 and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 provide only limited protection to persons dealing with companies which enter into contracts that are outside of their objects clauses or, ultra vires." You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case law in your answer.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an insolvent company restricted, the director's only hope of not being restricted is if he can show that he acted honestly and responsibly''. You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Clare and Maura are directors of, and 50:50 shareholders in, Retail… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Clare and Maura are directors of, and 50:50 shareholders in, Retail Rodents Limited (the "Company'') which operates the pet shop business, specialising in rabbits, domestic rats, mice and other small animals. The Company's shop is located in an affluent suburb in Cork and business has generally held up throughout the economic recession. Clare and Maura formed the Company after leaving secondary school. Of the two, Maura is more progressive and has tried on numerous occasions to improve the Company's business by marketing drives, but these have been negatively received by Clare who believes that marketing is wasteful and that most business comes from the fact their shop is close to a primary school, as children stop to look in the window and persuade their parents to buy pets for them. For a long time Maura believed that the internet had potential for the Company. Over a number of months Maura prepared a business plan around setting up a website, to be called Mouse-to-Mouse.ie, whereby people from County Cork can buy small pets online by clicking their picture which would then be delivered to them. Maura has projected that the venture would generate a profit of €75,000 per year and was very eager to proceed. On a number of occasions Maura tried to broach establishing a website, but on each occasion Clare's facial expressions made clear to Maura she was sceptical, and Maura never told her the full details or shared the business plan. Encouraged by her boyfriend, Simon, Maura eventually registered her own website which she called Mouse-to-Mouse.ie, and Simon delivered the purchased pets to customers. Maura and Simon set up their own company to run this business. Maura sourced the animals from the same breeders with whom she had built-up a relationship while acting as a director of Retail Rabbits Limited, and obtained the same volume discounts that Company had negotiated with them. As Maura had suspected, the venture was a success and after a year, was generating a profit of €90,000. Maura had never told Clare about her side-line, but when one of the breeders remarked to Clare that the Company's business must be booming due to the numbers of animals he had been supplying to Maura, Clare became suspicious. On confronting Maura and being told about the website and its success, Clare was outraged that Maura would have, as she saw it, abused her position in the Company by establishing a competing business. You are approached by Clare for advice as to whether Maura has been in breach of her duties as a director of the Company and if she has, as to what remedies are open to Clare and the Company. You are required to advise Clare and the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Sean, who is the liquidator of Timber Tables Limited (the "Company'')… Company.
questions.text: Sean, who is the liquidator of Timber Tables Limited (the "Company'') comes to you for advice. Sean tells you that the Company had traded as a small joinery making tables from wood before becoming insolvent. Before Sean can realise the Company's assets, he needs to establish what the Company owns. He has received a number of solicitors' letters from suppliers of goods to the Company demanding their return on the grounds that the contracts under which the goods were supplied provided that title to the goods did not pass to the Company. The cases in respect of which Sean wants your advice are: (a) Supplier A demands the return of all timber supplied to the Company, including that which has been used to make furniture by the Company. The retention of title in relation to the timber provided: "Title to the timber shall not pass to the Company until all sums due by the Company to Supplier A under any consignment of timber delivered to the Company have been paid in full". Supplier A claims that it is owed €150,000 from the Company for timber and that it is entitled to take that value in timber from the Company's storage and, because that is insufficient, to also take tables made from its timber from the showroom. (b) Supplier B demands the return of all bottles of specialised furniture oil which he supplied to the Company and also the proceeds of sale of any furniture oil sold for which the Company has not yet paid Supplier B. The retention of title clause in that case provided: "Ownership of the furniture oil supplied to the Company shall not pass until Supplier B has received in full the sums due to it from the Company and where the Company has sold furniture oil supplied by Supplier B, a trust in favour of Supplier B shall be impressed upon the proceeds of sale of that furniture oil." Supplier B is owed €40,000 by the Company and is claiming all of the remaining bottles of furniture oil on the premises, worth €20,000, as well as another €20,000 from the Company's bank account. (c) Neither Supplier A nor Supplier B delivered particulars of the foregoing contractual provisions to the Companies Registration Office for registration. You are required to advise Sean as to whether either of the foregoing claims are valid or whether Sean can raise section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 on the grounds that the contracts constitute unregistered charges. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law. Your answer must be in two parts and relate to the claims of Supplier A and Supplier B as described in (a) and (b). Each part carries equal marks.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement" You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to…
questions.text: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to transfer its shares, which restriction will usually take the form of either a right of pre-emption on transfer or a discretion vested in the directors to refuse the registration of share transfers". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Jason is a creditor of Dusty Deals Limited (the "Company'') which operated… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Jason is a creditor of Dusty Deals Limited (the "Company'') which operated an antique shop in Howth in the North of Dublin. Jason supplied over €100,000 worth of antiques to the Company for which he has not been paid. Jason had been pressing the Company's Managing Director and sole shareholder Fintan, for payment for some months but to no avail. Finally, Jason was contacted by Fintan and told that the Company was insolvent and that Fintan and his wife, the only other director, had resolved to put the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Jason heard that Fintan's daughter, Freda, opened her own antique shop in Dalkey in the South of Dublin. On visiting that premises, Jason recognised a number of antiques which he had sold to the Company (and for which he has not been paid). On confronting Freda, Jason was told that she had purchased all of her stock from the Company and if he had a gripe, he should take it up with her parents. Separately, Jason heard that another creditor of the Company who was the landlord of the Company's premises had been paid the rent owed to him by the Company and, coincidentally, was also the owner of the premises occupied by Freda in Dalkey. Jason believes that these transactions need to be investigated fully by the Company's liquidator but when Jason realised that the voluntary liquidator appointed by the sole member Fintan, is Dudley, he became very concerned and comes to you for advice before the creditors' meeting is held. Jason explains that Dudley is a first-cousin of Fintan who operates a small book-keeping business and Jason has serious doubts about his knowledge of company law, creditors' remedies and, indeed, the vigour with which Dudley would investigate the Company's affairs. You are required to advise Jason on: (a) Whether he can object to the appointment of Dudley as liquidator at the creditors' meeting and; (b) If Dudley were to be appointed liquidator, whether Jason could have the liquidation converted to an official liquidation. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company'') and holds 10%… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company'') and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing. Charlie's fellow directors, Richard and Martin each hold 45% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a printing press out of the Company to a friend of theirs in return for €50,000, which seems to be its market value. Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer. Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted; they also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see fit so he has little reason to be complaining. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company, and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.
